sentences.text: Mm-hmm, yeah.
Right.
This was still 19-- [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, I'm talking already-- I got in, already, to the 1942.
Yeah, this was already in fall 1942.

And then they started making ghettos, you know, to concentrate from all these small towns, and villages, and this, and even-- and the big cities, to put all these, you know, Jewish people in one area, that they wouldn't, you know-- a ghetto, that I'm sure you--
Pardon me?
The Germans, and they tried to clean out all the other places, to, to, to make unify, to take away all the Jews, and then to put them in the one area, and to have them there.
So we, we knew this, this started in different-- first, it started in Lwow, in the big city.
And then they started in the smaller.
So they took us to-- the, the nearest city was [INAUDIBLE].
And then they made, there, a ghetto, one part of the city.
And they fenced it around with wire, with this.
And then they, they, you know, tried all these Jewish people that there were in surrounding towns, to put them in there.
So this all-- yeah, this happened in sometimes in December.
And how did you know you had to go to the ghetto?
Because we knew-- because they started, like in Lwow, they started in other cities.
And we knew that that's what-- I mean, I guess they were clear that that's what they going to do.
They, they going to, to come-- I mean to take the Jewish people in one area, in certain areas, and have them all together.
I didn't go because that time my mother [INAUDIBLE] my parents were very concerned.
And they said, you know, we are older.
Whatever is going to happen, but we would like you and my oldest brother was that time with us.
And they were-- they were talking with some Gentiles that they lived, you know, like, next door.
And they liked us, and they didn't see-- they didn't want to see us, you know, being killed.
So he was afraid to, to make some arrangements for us, because it was next door, and, and it was suspicious.
If it would have been suspicious, you know, he was afraid that they might come and look.
So he made arrangements with another, with a brother-in-law, friend, I think, which lived a little bit away, a couple of blocks away from us.
And this was a few weeks before we knew that we have to leave-- that the ghetto-- that we have to go to the ghetto.
They, they, they went, they had-- it was a small house.
And there was a barn next to this house.
And under this barn, they dug out a, a bunk.
I don't know how you would call it here-- an, an area maybe?
I don't know, maybe it's 8 by 10, 6 by-- I don't know, something, underneath this barn.
And, and we were supposed to go in there and stay there, because we didn't-- I mean, everybody thought, maybe it's not going to last.
Everybody-- every time some, some news came, that the Russians are getting stronger, and this and that, and maybe they'll pull back.

So anyhow, the parents-- my parents, at that time, felt that, you know, whatever can be done for the time being, that we should remain in this village and not go to the ghetto, because the next step in ghetto was to, to surround and take away to the, to the gas chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING]
So anyhow, so we went there.
So my parents-- I don't know how, I guess that they had, still, a house in [INAUDIBLE] or something.
They went to, to the ghetto.
And they just-- there was nothing left, belongings to take, because the Ukrainians took everything away.
But we were-- we still had certain, few things.
And we, we gave it to, to another neighbor that we trusted that he would keep this, in case we need something.
We had a-- my mother had jewelry and things [INAUDIBLE].
And she told us, in case something happens to them, that she left there things that we can, you know, do something, to be able to, to survive for a little while.
So anyhow, they went to the ghetto.
And then we went to this hiding place.
And they were few more than we were.
About eight people there.
And it was a very, very tiny little place.
And we were just sitting like, you know, like this here.
There was no air.
And this sister-in-law of this man who, who gave us this, this, you know, the house, and so she used to cook food.
And she used to bring down every evening, and it was a small hole that-- to, to push in this, this food.
And we used to, you know, all have a few, a few spoons of soup or whatever the, you know, to just to be able to survive.
But after a few-- yeah, once a week, during the night, he used to, you know, let us come out of this, of this bunker, and to come into this barn there.
And then there was an attic to go up there and catch a little bit fresh air, because there was a time we couldn't-- there was not-- there was no air there.
I know either once a week or twice a week, but then it came to a point that-- I mean, I'm talking about myself.
I couldn't anymore, because my legs were so-- they were like rubber.
I, I-- my muscles had-- they didn't function.
We couldn't get up.
We couldn't stretch out.
We were just sitting, like in one position.
About eight, I think.
What is it?
Ah, we used to go up-- yeah, we used to go up.
But I don't know, once in two, three days, or whatever, where we couldn't anymore, you know, hold back.
And this was going on for a month.
And in between, in between this month that we were hiding there-- so all these people who came to the ghetto, I think, maybe a couple of weeks later they surrounded the ghetto.
And they cleaned out maybe half.
They took away my mother at that time, my father, and my oldest brother.
No, no, my oldest brother was with us.
I'm sorry, with me.
And they put them on a train, and they took them away.
So I remember this woman who used to make-- bring us down the food.
So she told us that they, they took away all the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and then I found out that they didn't take away all of them, because some were hiding.
Some weren't-- I mean, as many as they could, they gathered and they took them on the trains.
And then I found out that my, my father and mother were taken.
My aunts, and uncles, and cousins the same age as I was, they were there too.
They were, in the beginning, in Lwow.
But then they came to Hanaczow, and then they were, you know, [INAUDIBLE].
They went to the ghetto.
And they took them away, but my father managed-- and I don't know how it happened, but he jumped the train.
And he was the one who came back to the ghetto.
And one of my father's brothers, the, the half-brother, also came back.
His family, his children, everybody was gone.
And then they came back to the ghetto, which were much less than they started out with, maybe half or whatever.
And then they stayed, again, in the ghetto.
The same thing-- fear, and they didn't know what the next day's going to bring.
This woman, I found out.
And then also, they took some-- they needed some of the Jewish people.
They had, like, a Judenrate.
You know, that they need some Jewish people to, to-- when they needed something, you know, the Germans, they, they would have-- it, it was like a little-- what would you say?
Judenrate-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, how can I explain?

Like, like a little Jewish authority that they can come, and they can, you know, whatever they needed-- they needed money, they needed jewelry, they needed whatever, that the Germans have somebody to come to.
So one of our friends was one of this-- a member of this Judenrate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he was-- he was hiding with us, in the beginning, too.
Or, or I can't remember exactly.
Either was, he was with us, or, no.
I think he-- they made him a part of the Judenrate before we got into hiding.
But his wife was still with us, and his mother and two sisters.
But then, being that he was free, it's like he could be free.
He didn't have to be in, in ghetto all the time because he had to take care of certain things that the Germans wanted him to.
What type of things?
Like when they needed certain things from the Jews, so they came to this Judenrate, you know.
Zevel Gruder, G-R-U-D-E-R.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So being that he was free to go to this [INAUDIBLE], and come back to Hanaczow, and go places, so one night, he came.
And I just felt it.
I cannot-- -- continue there.
So he said, you know what?
I'll take you to [INAUDIBLE], back to the ghetto.
And this, I felt, no matter what is going to happen, I'm not going to be able to, to, to survive here much longer, in this.
And I don't think they was-- they were willing to keep us much longer.
What was the name of those people?
His name was Igdance Horneak.
This wasn't a wife.
This was his sister-in-law.
Yeah, but they lived in the same house.
The Polish name was [INAUDIBLE] Horneak, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So then I went back to the ghetto.
He took me there.
I couldn't walk, I couldn't breathe, and it took quite a few-- a week or so.
And then I felt, I don't want to be-- I mean, my father, that time, and there was still my mother's sister there.
And they said, do something.
Get away from here.
Don't be in ghetto.
So I tried to get back to the concentration camp that I worked the summer before.
You follow me?
[LAUGHS] Why did you want to go back to the concentration camp?
Because that time, we knew that the concentration camps are going to go on a little longer.
The ghettos, we knew they're going to be cleaned out.
And everybody tried to get into the concentration camps, because they needed labor, you know?
They needed people to work on the, on the [INAUDIBLE], on the street, on the roads.
And so you know, everybody tried, you know to, to, to, to stretch, to see.
Maybe some miracle is going to happen in between.
But we know that the, the ghettos are going to be finished off.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when I gained a little strength and I could start, you know, using my legs again, so I went back.
I got dressed.
It was very cold that time.
It was in January or something like that.
And I-- you know, they wouldn't see my face, that they wouldn't, you know, recognize that I'm Jewish.
And I started walking through the fields and through the roads, and go back to Hanaczow.
And then I stopped there, and I sleep-- I slept through one or two nights.
I can't remember exactly.
And I, a farmer's house, that he had some, oh, stacks of hay.
So I, I got in there.
I made a little, little, a little hole.
I got them there.
And I stayed through one night, and then that came out.
One of these farmers, [INAUDIBLE] Gentile, came out.
And he saw me there, and he was very-- you know, he didn't know what to do.
And he didn't want, you know, make me run away, because during-- during the day, he brought out a little food, and I ate.
And then, I guess, the next day-- or maybe I stayed through another night.
And then I went to Kuriwice, to this camp where I worked this-- the summer before.
And that time, they had already facilities for women.
It was a house.
They took over a house, a big house.
And they were-- I don't know how many of use were there, but mostly-- there weren't too many.
Mostly, we worked there on, on the field, not on the road, but on the field.
And so anyhow, I, I tried to get there, back.
And that time, it was very difficult.
They didn't want to take me back there because this previous summer we were just like day workers and go home.
And that time, a lot of-- from Lwow, a lot of young woman started to get in there because they were running away from the ghetto.
Everybody wanted to run away from the ghetto.
So they took in a lot of these women that they had nothing to do with these concentration camp before.
And they said, there is no place for me, and this and that.

But then the guy who was supervising the, the labor on this farm there, with, you know, with all these things that we took care of-- so he felt very bad for me because he felt that I worked so hard, you know, just the summer before.
And now I came back, and I, and I was sick, and couldn't walk, and this.
And he said, now, [INAUDIBLE] we'll get you in, and he was Ukrainian.
He was the one that this Lagerfuhrer lived in his house, because he had a very nice house like a villa.
And he lived a there, and this, this Ukrainian who was supervising the work day on the fields, he lived, like, in one room, and he had a big basement.
And he kept quite a few of us in the basement.
And he said, we'll see.
They have to take you in, back to the-- it's a-- it's one-- it's a part of the, of the concentration camp, because he kept us like, you know, that nobody would know, because he wasn't allowed to.
Nozarki, yeah.
There were about six, and he kept us in the basement there.
And we did some work, and-- and then in the same time, they changed Lagerfuhrers, you know, because the summer before was [INAUDIBLE], and then they, they took him away, and they brought another one.
And this other one, Kemke was his name, was supposed to be a much-- you know, a little bit, you know, less aggressive.
The other [INAUDIBLE] was very, very aggressive and very strict.
And he said, we'll wait and see.
We have to get you back into camp, to the concentration camps of [INAUDIBLE].
In the meanwhile, I came down with typhus fever.
And when I lived-- when I was in the basement in his house, I came down with a very high temperature.
And I was there for about three days.

And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't stay there anymore, because this was a very infectious-- you know, it was, anyway, already-- practically everybody went through this, from the not being clean or what.
I don't know.
I think lice or whatever.
They said that lice bring.
I didn't happen to have them, but they bring the infection, you know.
Anyhow, I came down with high temperature.
And he-- they couldn't keep me there, and, and these-- there was a few Jewish guys that were also supervising this field work.
You know, they're-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they, they were very nice.
And they also could travel between Kurowice and [INAUDIBLE].
And they took me back to the ghetto.
And there was a hospital that they used to take in all these sick people.
And I said, I don't want to go to the hospital.
And they didn't want to put me there, because many times they came and whoever was with all these infectious disease, they used to, you know-- they used to finish them off there.
They used to kill them there.
So they took me back to-- my father was still in, in this place where he lived, that I don't know how many families lived in this place.
And he took me there, and I was there for about 14 days, this took, you know, this high temperature and all this was going on.
And nothing to eat, and I couldn't eat, I couldn't drink.
Anyway, it's-- I was just-- you know, this was for 14 days.
And my father was there, and my aunt-- my mother's sister-- was there.
And they nursed me and mostly, you know, liquids, liquids, you know.
And when I got through, when the temperature went down, I think after-- it takes 14 days or something.
I could not-- again, I could not walk.
I could not-- I, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- my legs were just like bone and skin.
My arms, it just-- I was just like a skeleton after this because it happened a little bit before, during this hiding, and then [INAUDIBLE] you know, during this time that I was sick.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they started, you know, they started helping me, you know, to walk a little bit.
They held me under my arms until I could, you know, walk around a little bit.
And then I had very long hair.
And then [INAUDIBLE] they had to wash my hair.
Then all my hair was out in this ball, you know, because after the sickness, you lose all your hair.
And, and this was going on for about two or three weeks.
I couldn't-- I could hardly walk on my feet.
But then, one of-- [INAUDIBLE] in concentration-- I mean in the-- in ghetto came.
And they said, there's rumors that the ghetto is going to be surrounded, and they're going to finish off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So during the night I still-- I could hardly walk that time.
So my father and I-- this was, maybe, in February.
It was cold, and it was slick, ice, and snow, and mud, and all kind of-- so he dragged me by my arm, and we were walking and walking out from this, from the ghetto.
We sort of, like, escaping out from this little [INAUDIBLE], from [INAUDIBLE], and started walking back to Hanaczow.
And then I stayed there a few days again.
In Hanaczow?
Yeah, also.
Pardon me?
Also a [INAUDIBLE] you know, like outdoors, you know, where they had these stacks with hay and straw.
I was sitting there.
And then there was another family there, that they were also, like-- they live still in Hanaczow, Jewish people, but they were also, like, in the Judenrate.
And then they took me in for a couple days, and there was sort of like a place in their little house, their little room, that I could hide.
And they kept me there, I think, also a few days.
And then I went back to the concentration camp again.
You know, bundled myself up, and this was, again, about eight miles, nine miles, you know?
And I walked there, and I came there.
And then they tried to push me back into the, to this, to be a part of the concentration camp.
And then I remained there till a week before they liquidated it.
What was it like, the concentration camp?
Working every day, in the field.
[INAUDIBLE] came to springtime, we started planting and, and making ready the, the soil, and, and working every day there.
And many times, we were afraid to go back to this house to sleep.

But being that we worked on the fields, sometimes we-- you know, if nobody knew, we stayed there during the night too, that we wouldn't go [INAUDIBLE] because it was [INAUDIBLE] always a frightening feeling.
Is they going to come during the night or something?
They're going to finish us off.
And this was going on till, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] until June, I think, June and July.
They used to-- you know, we used to go down to the, to the main camp.
And they had loafs of bread.
Like, I don't know how big, it's like a loaf like this.
They used to-- everybody used to get, like, a eighth of-- you know, like they cut it up in eight pieces.
And so they used to sometimes choose me to go down and bring up the, the bread.
And then they used to make some soup there, and to bring out the soup on the field, that we would, you know, eat there.
Sometimes we had to go down to the, to the kitchen, to the dining room there, [INAUDIBLE] whatever, to the main-- and a lot of times, they brought up on the, on the field, and we had there.
But it was so horrible, this food, that I [INAUDIBLE] when I happened to see how they made this food in the evening, it was so bad, and I was so hungry and this.
But I couldn't eat this food.
They used to take, you know, leaves from, from the cabbage.
And there were big worms there.
They used to chop it up, you know, and make a soup out of it, and [INAUDIBLE].
So there is a lot, a lot in between that, you know, is just-- it's just hard to explain everything, you know?
And it's such a long time.
But I'm just telling you what comes to my mind, to what I can remember more vivid.
And just a week, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, there was a lot of talk because they, they terminated, already, one concentration camp there nearby.
They terminated another one, and then-- and [INAUDIBLE] they terminated [INAUDIBLE].
Then we knew that any time, we're going to be next.
So--
Yeah, sure, sure.
